content:
Hi!

I just compiled with Rust support.

Then I run with:
qemu-system-x86_64 -kernel arch/x86_64/boot/bzImage -initrd ramdisk.img -nographic -m 512 -append console=ttyS0

How to test something related to Rust? =)
Loading a module?

This qemu command that posted shows in the end:

No root device specified. Boot arguments must include a root= parameter.


BusyBox v1.30.1 (Ubuntu 1:1.30.1-4ubuntu6.4) built-in shell (ash)
Enter 'help' for a list of built-in commands.

Which command do you use to run qemu, please?
The CI we use may help you (see ci.yaml), though there are likely many other articles online that do a better job explaining all the details. See also the QEMU docs etc.

My current way of booting and developing for the kernel is an archlinux virtual machine, in which I build and install the kernel, since I figured I would like to have a root file sytem etc.
I use this guid to configure the system.
I would like to hear about the dev setups of others to improve my workflow.

Leon Gross said:

My current way of booting and developing for the kernel is an archlinux virtual machine, in which I build and install the kernel, since I figured I would like to have a root file sytem etc.
I use this guid to configure the system.
I would like to hear about the dev setups of others to improve my workflow.

Thank you Leon!
I'll try that guide.

You may try to generate a complete Linux image with, for instance, Buildroot. That should move the building process to the host side. Buildroot is a relatively simple and flexible tool. I prefer it for testing with a VM.

Maciej Falkowski said:

You may try to generate a complete Linux image with, for instance, Buildroot. That should move the building process to the host side. Buildroot is a relatively simple and flexible tool. I prefer it for testing with a VM.

I would like to try this out, would you mind sharing your buildroot config?

Leon Gross said:

I would like to try this out, would you mind sharing your buildroot config?

I have detailed the process here: https://github.com/d0u9/Linux-Device-Driver/tree/draft/02_getting_start_with_driver_development

It seems to me that the config very much depends on the context and what you are dealing with.
Perhaps the best option is to generate your own.
